questions.question: (i) How many bits make up one byte? (ii) Name the unit of data that consists of 4 bits. [2]
questions.model_answer: (i) One byte is made up of 8 bits. (ii) A unit of 4 bits is called a nibble.

questions.question: Convert the hexadecimal number A2F: (i) into binary, and (ii) into denary. [4]
Add the two 8-bit binary integers 01001101 and 00101110. Give your answer in binary and state whether overflow has occurred. [3]
questions.model_answer: 01001101 (77) + 00101110 (46) = 01111011, which is 123 in denary. The result fits within 8 bits, so no overflow has occurred (overflow would occur only if the result were too large to store in the 8 available bits).

questions.question: Explain the difference between lossy and lossless compression. [2]
questions.model_answer: Lossy compression permanently removes some data to reduce the file size, so the original file cannot be fully restored; it is often used for images, audio, and video. Lossless compression reduces the file size without removing any data, so the original file can be restored exactly; it is used where all data must be kept, such as text files.

questions.question: Explain how an image is represented in binary and describe how colour depth and resolution each affect the file size. [5]
questions.model_answer: An image is made up of a grid of pixels, and the colour of each pixel is stored as a binary number. The colour depth is the number of bits used to represent each pixel: a greater colour depth allows more colours but uses more bits per pixel, increasing the file size. The resolution is the number of pixels in the image (width x height): a higher resolution means more pixels, which also increases the file size. The total file size can be estimated by multiplying the number of pixels by the colour depth in bits.

questions.question: Describe the difference between a compiler and an interpreter. [2]
questions.model_answer: A compiler translates the whole high-level program into machine code in one go before it is run, producing an executable file. An interpreter translates and executes the program one line (or statement) at a time, stopping when it reaches an error.
